always.why: Baseline for every knee assessment; screens effusion, quadriceps wasting, varus/valgus alignment, and antalgic gait.

always.exam: Active + passive knee ROM (flexion, extension) with end-feel
always.why: Range and end-feel is required to identify a capsular/effusion-limited pattern versus an extension block (meniscal/loose body/ACL) or extensor-mechanism lag.
always.confirmsOrExcludes: Screens capsular pattern, extension block, extensor lag

always.exam: Ligament stability screen (Lachman's, valgus/varus stress) + meniscal screen (McMurray's)
always.why: First-line provocation cluster to broadly screen the four major ligaments and the menisci before drilling into a specific mechanism.

add.exam: Lachman's test + anterior drawer + pivot shift test
add.why: A non-contact twisting/pivoting mechanism is the classic ACL injury pattern and warrants the full ACL test cluster.
A felt/heard pop at the time of injury is a recognised ACL-rupture flag and should be confirmed with the most sensitive ACL test.

add.exam: Lachman's + pivot shift test; screen for osteochondral fracture / patellar dislocation
add.why: Immediate (within 2 hours) haemarthrosis strongly implicates ACL rupture, osteochondral fracture, or patellar dislocation and needs urgent differentiation.
add.confirmsOrExcludes: Confirms/excludes ACL tear vs osteochondral fracture vs patellar dislocation

add.why: Giving way specifically with pivoting/direction change is a hallmark of ACL-deficient rotational instability.

add.exam: Valgus stress test at 0° and 30° flexion (MCL)
add.why: A direct blow to the medial knee (valgus stress mechanism) is the classic MCL injury pattern.

add.exam: Varus stress test at 0° and 30° flexion (LCL)
add.why: A direct blow to the lateral knee (varus stress mechanism) is the classic LCL injury pattern.

add.exam: Posterior drawer test + observe for posterior tibial sag
add.why: A dashboard mechanism or direct blow to the anterior tibia is the classic PCL injury pattern; posterior sag must be checked before anterior drawer to avoid misinterpretation.

add.exam: McMurray's test + Apley's grind test + Thessaly test (meniscal cluster)
add.why: Mechanical catching/clicking/grinding at the joint line warrants the full meniscal provocation cluster.

add.exam: Full passive ROM block assessment + meniscal cluster; consider urgent orthopaedic referral if irreducible
add.why: True mechanical locking (cannot fully extend) suggests a displaced meniscal fragment (bucket-handle tear) or loose body and may need urgent referral if irreducible.
add.confirmsOrExcludes: Confirms/excludes locked meniscal tear / loose body

add.exam: Clarke's sign + patellar grind test + patellar mobility/tracking assessment
add.why: The movie sign (pain with prolonged knee flexion) is a classic patellofemoral pain syndrome marker and should be corroborated with patellofemoral provocation tests.

add.exam: Clarke's sign + patellar grind test + patellar mobility/tracking assessment
add.why: Pain worse descending stairs (higher patellofemoral joint reaction force) is a classic patellofemoral pattern and should be corroborated with patellofemoral provocation tests.

add.exam: Clarke's sign + patellar grind test + patellar mobility/tracking assessment
add.why: Diffuse anterior/peripatellar pain without a clear traumatic mechanism is the classic patellofemoral pain syndrome presentation.

add.exam: Resisted knee extension (MMT) + palpate patellar tendon inferior pole
add.why: Inferior-pole patellar tendon pain in a jumping/loading athlete is the classic patellar tendinopathy pattern.
Lateral knee pain at the ITB attachment in a runner suggests iliotibial band friction syndrome and should be corroborated with the Noble compression test and Ober's test for ITB tightness.

add.exam: Palpate medial joint line + McMurray's test (medial technique)
add.why: Medial joint line pain warrants direct palpation and the medial McMurray's technique to screen the medial meniscus / MCL.
add.confirmsOrExcludes: Confirms/excludes medial meniscal or MCL source

add.exam: Palpate lateral joint line + McMurray's test (lateral technique)
add.why: Lateral joint line pain warrants direct palpation and the lateral McMurray's technique to screen the lateral meniscus / LCL.
add.confirmsOrExcludes: Confirms/excludes lateral meniscal or LCL source

add.exam: Sweep/ballottement test for effusion; consider knee OA / meniscal screen
add.why: A delayed or recurrent effusion pattern (reactive synovitis) points toward meniscal pathology or early osteoarthritis rather than an acute ligamentous rupture.

supporting.exam: Neurological screen — L3 (knee extension) / S2 (knee flexion) myotomes + patella reflex if radiculopathy suspected
supporting.why: Rules out a lumbar nerve-root contribution when knee pain has an atypical or referred quality.

supporting.exam: Functional + outcome measure (KOOS, Lysholm, IKDC, or Kujala score for PFPS)
supporting.why: Quantifies baseline knee-related disability for goal-setting and re-assessment.

supporting.exam: Imaging (Ottawa Knee Rules if acute trauma; MRI only if red flag or failed conservative management)
supporting.why: Plain film is guided by the Ottawa Knee Rules after acute trauma; MRI is reserved for red flags, suspected internal derangement, or failure to progress with conservative care.
supporting.confirmsOrExcludes: Confirms structural pathology when clinically indicated
